name: Bangladesh
tagline: From Buddhist Bengal and the wealth of the Mughals to the nation born in 1971.
summary: The deltaic land of Bengal is one of the oldest cradles of civilisation in South Asia, ruled in turn by the Buddhist Pala emperors, the Hindu Senas, an independent Muslim sultanate and the fabulously wealthy Mughal province whose Dhaka muslin clothed the courts of the world. British conquest after the Battle of Plassey in 1757 reduced this rich land to a colonial periphery scarred by catastrophic famine. Partition in 1947 made it East Pakistan, but linguistic and political grievance — crystallised by the 1952 Language Movement and the 1970 election — erupted into the Liberation War of 1971. On 26 March 1971 independence was proclaimed and on 16 December the Pakistani army surrendered, founding the People's Republic of Bangladesh under Sheikh Mujibur Rahman.

founding.label: Liberation War and independence
founding.detail: Bangladesh declared independence from Pakistan on 26 March 1971, after the Pakistani army launched a brutal crackdown on the Bengali nationalist movement led by Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, whose Awami League had won the 1970 election. After nine months of war and the flight of some ten million refugees to India, Indian forces intervened and the Pakistani army surrendered at Dhaka on 16 December 1971, the date now commemorated as Victory Day. The new state's civilisational roots, however, reach back more than a thousand years to the Buddhist Pala empire of Bengal.

eras.title: Buddhist and Hindu Bengal: the Pala and Sena Dynasties
eras.standfirst: For nearly five centuries Bengal was ruled by the great Buddhist Pala emperors and then the Hindu Senas, an age of monasteries, learning and the splendour of Somapura.
body: In the mid-eighth century, after a period of disorder, a chieftain named Gopala was raised to power and founded the Pala dynasty, which would rule Bihar and Bengal from the 8th to the 12th century. The Palas were devout patrons of Buddhism, and it was through missionaries from their kingdom that Buddhism was finally established in Tibet. Under Pala patronage a distinctive school of art arose, of which many noteworthy sculptures in stone and metal survive, and great centres of monastic learning flourished across the delta.

The supreme monument of this Buddhist Bengal is the Somapura Mahavihara at Paharpur, built by the second Pala king, Dharmapala, around the turn of the ninth century. One of the largest Buddhist monasteries south of the Himalayas, it drew scholars and monks from across Asia and remained a renowned intellectual centre until the 12th century. Its cruciform plan, perfectly adapted to its religious function, represents a unique artistic achievement; UNESCO inscribed the ruins as a World Heritage Site in 1985.

The last important Pala king, Ramapala, did much to strengthen the dynasty, but on his death Pala power was eclipsed by the rising Senas, a Hindu dynasty that united Bengal under a Brahmanical order in the twelfth century. The Sena age was a brief but brilliant Hindu revival in literature and religion. It ended abruptly: around 1204 the Turkish general Bakhtiyar Khalji swept into Bengal, overthrowing Sena rule and opening the land to a new faith and a new political order.

events.title: Gopala founds the Pala dynasty
events.summary: Gopala is raised to power amid disorder and founds the Pala dynasty, a Buddhist imperial line that would rule Bengal and Bihar for some four centuries and spread Buddhism as far as Tibet.

events.title: Somapura Mahavihara built at Paharpur
events.summary: The Pala king Dharmapala builds the great monastery of Somapura at Paharpur, one of the largest Buddhist viharas south of the Himalayas and a centre of learning for centuries; today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

events.title: Turkish conquest ends Sena rule
events.summary: The general Bakhtiyar Khalji conquers Bengal, ending the Hindu Sena dynasty and inaugurating centuries of Muslim rule in the delta.

figures.name: Dharmapala
figures.blurb: The second and one of the greatest Pala kings, a champion of Buddhism who founded the Somapura Mahavihara at Paharpur, the supreme monument of Buddhist Bengal.

pullquote.text: A renowned intellectual centre until the 12th century, its layout perfectly adapted to its religious function.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO on the Somapura Mahavihara at Paharpur

eras.title: The Bengal Sultanate and the Spread of Islam
eras.standfirst: An independent Muslim kingdom emerged from Delhi's shadow, and through Sufi saints and settlers Islam took deep root in the Bengali countryside.
body: After the Turkish conquest, Bengal was loosely tied to the Delhi Sultanate, but its distance from Delhi and the difficulty of the riverine terrain encouraged independence. In 1342 Shamsuddin Iliyas Shah defeated his rivals, ascended the throne at Lakhnauti, and founded the Iliyas Shahi dynasty, consolidating an independent Sultanate of Bengal that would endure, under several dynasties, for more than two centuries. By campaigning across the delta he became master of the whole of Bengal.

Islam, which had come in the wake of the Turkish conquest, gradually transformed the social and religious fabric of the land. Muslim immigrants — traders, travellers and preachers — settled in the cities of Gaur, Pandua, Satgaon and Sonargaon, while Sufi saints established hospices in the countryside, reclaiming land and teaching new farming techniques to marginal communities. This patient process explains the eventual emergence of a Muslim majority in the eastern delta that would one day become Bangladesh.

The sultans were significant patrons of culture, fostering the growth of the Bengali language and a syncretic court life that drew on Persian, Arabic and indigenous traditions. Under Husain Shah (reigned 1494–1519) and his Husain Shahi successors the sultanate reached a cultural and territorial height. Independent Bengal finally fell when the Mughal emperor Akbar's armies defeated the last sultan at the Battle of Rajmahal in 1576, drawing the delta into the Mughal Empire.

events.title: Iliyas Shah founds the Bengal Sultanate
events.summary: Shamsuddin Iliyas Shah unifies Bengal's principalities and establishes the independent Iliyas Shahi Sultanate at Lakhnauti, beginning more than two centuries of sovereign Muslim rule.

events.title: Reign of Husain Shah
events.summary: Alauddin Husain Shah presides over a golden age of the Bengal Sultanate, patronising Bengali literature and extending the kingdom's frontiers.

events.title: Mughal conquest of Bengal
events.summary: Akbar's forces defeat the last independent sultan at the Battle of Rajmahal, ending the Bengal Sultanate and absorbing the delta into the Mughal Empire.

figures.name: Shamsuddin Iliyas Shah
figures.blurb: The ruler who unified Bengal's warring principalities into a single independent sultanate and gave its people, for the first time, a common Bengali Muslim political identity.

eras.title: Mughal Bengal and the Wealth of the Delta
eras.standfirst: As a Mughal province, Bengal became the empire's richest jewel — a land of rice, silk and the legendary muslin of Dhaka, woven so fine it was called woven air.
body: Under Mughal rule Bengal became the wealthiest province (subah) of the empire, its fertile delta producing vast surpluses of rice, sugar, silk and cotton. In 1610 the Mughal governor moved the provincial capital to Dhaka, which grew into one of the great commercial cities of Asia and the operational base for imperial campaigns into Assam. Bengal's revenues were the financial engine of the Mughal state, and its riverine ports drew Portuguese, Dutch, French and English traders.

The most fabled of Bengal's products was Dhaka muslin, a cotton fabric of extraordinary lightness and transparency prized by Mughal emperors and European nobility alike and renowned across the world as 'woven air'. During the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries Mughal Bengal was the foremost muslin exporter on earth, and the delta as a whole accounted for a large share of European trade with Asia — Bengal alone supplied a substantial part of Dutch imports from the continent.

As Mughal central authority weakened after the death of Aurangzeb in 1707, the governors of Bengal became effectively autonomous rulers, the nawabs. Wealthy, populous and only loosely tethered to a fading Delhi, the Bengal of the early eighteenth century was a glittering prize. It was precisely this prosperity that drew the ambitions of the European trading companies, and above all the English East India Company, whose fortified factory at Calcutta would soon bring it into open conflict with the nawab.

events.title: Dhaka becomes the Mughal capital of Bengal
events.summary: The Mughal governor establishes Dhaka as the capital of the Bengal subah, and the city grows into a hub of administration, military power and the global muslin trade.

events.title: Dhaka muslin and the wealth of Bengal
events.summary: Mughal Bengal becomes the world's foremost exporter of fine muslin and a leading source of European trade with Asia, making the province the richest in the empire.

events.title: The nawabs of Bengal become autonomous
events.summary: As Mughal central power fades after Aurangzeb's death, the governors of Bengal rule as near-independent nawabs over the empire's wealthiest province.

pullquote.text: Renowned as 'woven air', Dhaka muslin was prized by Mughal emperors and European nobility alike.
pullquote.attribution: On the textile wealth of Mughal Bengal

eras.title: British Conquest and Colonial Bengal
eras.standfirst: Plassey delivered Bengal to a trading company; what followed was famine, deindustrialisation, and the partitions that began to redraw the map of the delta.
body: On 23 June 1757 the East India Company army under Robert Clive defeated Siraj-ud-Daulah, the last independent nawab of Bengal, at the Battle of Plassey — a victory secured less by force than by the treachery of the nawab's commander Mir Jafar, who had been bribed to hold back his troops. The defeat handed the Company effective control of Bengal's revenues and is conventionally taken to mark the beginning of British political dominance over the whole of India.

Company rule brought ruin to a once-rich province. In 1769–70 the Great Bengal Famine killed an estimated ten million people — roughly a third of the population — as monsoon failure combined with the Company's refusal to relax its land-revenue demands even as peasants starved. Over the following century Bengal's celebrated textile industry was undercut and dismantled in favour of British manufactures, and the wealthiest province of the Mughal world was reduced to a colonial supplier of raw jute and a periphery of empire.

Bengal also became a crucible of resistance. In 1905 the viceroy Lord Curzon partitioned the province into a Hindu-majority west and a Muslim-majority east with its capital at Dacca, ostensibly for administrative ease but with the effect of dividing the population along religious lines. The move provoked the Swadeshi movement and a wave of nationalist agitation that forced its annulment in 1911 — but it had planted the idea of a separate Muslim-majority eastern Bengal that would resurface, decisively, four decades later.

events.title: Battle of Plassey
events.summary: Robert Clive's East India Company forces defeat Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah after the betrayal of Mir Jafar, beginning British political control of Bengal and, in time, of India.

events.title: The Great Bengal Famine
events.summary: An estimated ten million people — about a third of Bengal's population — die as monsoon failure compounds the East India Company's insistence on collecting land revenue from a starving peasantry.

events.title: First Partition of Bengal
events.summary: Viceroy Lord Curzon divides Bengal into a Muslim-majority east (capital Dacca) and a Hindu-majority west, sparking the Swadeshi movement; the partition is annulled in 1911.

figures.name: Siraj-ud-Daulah
figures.blurb: The young nawab who resisted the East India Company's encroachments and was defeated and killed after the Battle of Plassey, the betrayal of his own commanders sealing the fall of independent Bengal.

pullquote.text: An estimated ten million people, nearly a third of Bengal's population, perished in the famine of 1770.
pullquote.attribution: On the human cost of early Company rule

eras.title: Partition, East Pakistan and the Language Movement
eras.standfirst: The 1947 partition made the eastern delta a wing of Pakistan a thousand miles from its capital; the struggle to defend the Bengali language lit the fuse of a nation.
body: When British India was partitioned in August 1947, Muslim-majority eastern Bengal became East Pakistan, the eastern wing of a new state whose two halves were separated by more than a thousand miles of Indian territory and divided by language, culture and economy. Power and investment were concentrated in West Pakistan, and Bengalis — though a majority of Pakistan's population — quickly came to feel like a neglected and exploited periphery within their own country.

The first great grievance was language. When the central government moved to make Urdu the sole state language of Pakistan, Bengalis, more than half the population, resisted. On 21 February 1952 students in Dhaka defied a ban on demonstrations to demand that Bengali be recognised as a state language; police opened fire, killing several, among them Abul Barkat, Rafiq Uddin Ahmed and Abdul Jabbar. The martyrs of the Language Movement became sacred to Bengali nationalism, and in 1999 UNESCO proclaimed 21 February International Mother Language Day in their memory.

The Language Movement transformed cultural identity into political demand. Through the 1950s and 1960s the Awami League, led by Sheikh Mujibur Rahman, channelled Bengali resentment into a programme of autonomy, embodied in its Six-Point demand of 1966 for self-government of the eastern wing. Repression and economic neglect deepened, and the catastrophic Bhola cyclone of November 1970 — which killed hundreds of thousands and was met with a sluggish central response — convinced many Bengalis that only self-rule could secure their future.

events.title: Partition creates East Pakistan
events.summary: The partition of British India makes Muslim-majority eastern Bengal into East Pakistan, a wing of the new state separated from its western half by a thousand miles of India.

events.title: The Bengali Language Movement
events.summary: Students in Dhaka demanding that Bengali be a state language of Pakistan are shot by police; the martyrs become foundational to Bengali nationalism and, later, to UNESCO's International Mother Language Day.

events.title: The Awami League's Six-Point demand
events.summary: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman's Awami League issues a six-point programme for the political and economic autonomy of East Pakistan, the charter of Bengali self-determination.

figures.name: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman
figures.role: Leader of the Awami League; founding father of Bangladesh
figures.blurb: A co-founder of the Awami League and a superb orator jailed repeatedly by Pakistan's rulers, 'Bangabandhu' (Friend of Bengal) became the embodiment of Bengali nationalism and would lead his people to independence.

pullquote.text: On 21 February 1952, students defied a ban to demand their mother tongue, and several were shot dead.
pullquote.attribution: The martyrs of the Bengali Language Movement

eras.title: Liberation War and Modern Bangladesh
eras.standfirst: An election denied, a war of independence, and the long, turbulent making of a nation — from famine and coups to a garment-powered economy and the uprising of 2024.
body: In December 1970 the Awami League won a sweeping victory, taking 167 of the 169 East Pakistani seats and an outright majority in Pakistan's National Assembly. When West Pakistan's leaders refused to let Mujibur Rahman form a government, Bengal rose in protest. On the night of 25 March 1971 the Pakistani army launched Operation Searchlight, a savage crackdown targeting students, intellectuals and the Hindu minority; on 26 March, shortly before his arrest, Mujib proclaimed Bangladesh's independence. Mukti Bahini guerrillas fought a nine-month war as some ten million refugees fled into India.

Britannica records that possibly a million Bengalis were killed in the conflict, which ended when India intervened in December and the Pakistani army surrendered at Dhaka on 16 December 1971, founding the People's Republic of Bangladesh. The new nation's start was harsh: war damage and the failures of relief produced a devastating famine in 1974, and on 15 August 1975 Mujib and most of his family were assassinated in a military coup. A counter-coup in November brought Ziaur Rahman to power; he founded the Bangladesh Nationalist Party and ruled until his own assassination in 1981, followed by the long military presidency of H. M. Ershad until 1990.

Civilian democracy returned in 1991, settling into a fierce rivalry between the Awami League under Mujib's daughter Sheikh Hasina and the BNP under Zia's widow Khaleda Zia. Over these decades Bangladesh transformed economically: a ready-made garment industry employing some four to five million workers, most of them women, made the country the world's second-largest clothing exporter and contributed roughly a tenth of its GDP. Hasina governed for fifteen unbroken years from 2009 until a student-led mass uprising over a job-quota system drove her from power and into exile in India on 5 August 2024, after which the Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus led an interim government charged with reform and fresh elections.

events.title: Awami League wins the election
events.summary: The Awami League secures 167 of the 169 East Pakistani seats and a National Assembly majority; West Pakistan's refusal to hand over power ignites the crisis that leads to war.

events.title: Declaration of independence
events.summary: After the Pakistani army launches Operation Searchlight on 25 March, Sheikh Mujibur Rahman proclaims Bangladesh's independence on 26 March, beginning the Liberation War.

events.title: Victory and the birth of Bangladesh
events.summary: After a nine-month war in which possibly a million Bengalis were killed and ten million fled to India, Indian forces intervene and the Pakistani army surrenders at Dhaka, founding the People's Republic of Bangladesh.

events.title: Assassination of Mujib and military rule
events.summary: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman and most of his family are killed in a coup; a counter-coup brings Ziaur Rahman to power, beginning fifteen years of military-dominated rule.

events.title: The 2024 uprising and Hasina's fall
events.summary: A student-led mass uprising over a civil-service job-quota system forces Sheikh Hasina to resign after fifteen years and flee to India; the Nobel laureate Muhammad Yunus is appointed to head an interim government.

figures.name: Ziaur Rahman
figures.role: President of Bangladesh; founder of the BNP
figures.blurb: An army officer of the Liberation War who took power after the 1975 coup, became president in 1977 and founded the Bangladesh Nationalist Party, emphasising food production and rural development before his assassination in 1981.

figures.name: Sheikh Hasina Wazed
figures.blurb: A surviving daughter of Sheikh Mujibur Rahman who led the Awami League and governed Bangladesh for fifteen unbroken years, overseeing rapid economic growth before being driven from power by the 2024 uprising.

pullquote.text: On March 26, shortly before he was arrested and moved to West Pakistan, Mujibur Rahman declared Bangladesh's independence.

figures.name: Sheikh Mujibur Rahman
figures.role: Founding father and first leader of Bangladesh (1920–1975)
figures.blurb: Bangabandhu, leader of the Awami League, who declared independence in 1971 and became the new nation's first prime minister before his assassination in 1975.

figures.name: Ziaur Rahman
figures.blurb: Liberation War officer who took power after 1975, founded the Bangladesh Nationalist Party and led the country until his assassination in 1981.

figures.name: Dharmapala
figures.role: Pala emperor of Bengal (reigned c. 781–821 CE)
figures.blurb: Buddhist ruler who founded the Somapura Mahavihara at Paharpur, the greatest monument of early Bengal and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

sources.label: Ruins of the Buddhist Vihara at Paharpur
Gearing Up for the Future of Manufacturing in Bangladesh
